---
title: "FAQ & Troubleshooting"
---

## Why does `scfit()` require at least one Z column?

The DML estimator is defined in terms of a nuisance function
$\hat\beta(\mathbf Z)$ that maps respondent-level covariates into
preference vectors. Without any moderators the mapping is trivial
and the DML correction degenerates to a plain plug-in estimator ---
users who truly want a homogeneous estimator should fit a standard
`glm(choice ~ deltaX, family = binomial)` instead of the structural
DNN.

## How do I know my `sc_fit` is well-trained?

`summary(fit)` reports the DML/iid SE ratio. Values close to one
indicate that the clustering correction is small and the DNN's
per-respondent $\hat\beta$ has little additional within-respondent
information beyond the population mean. Per-fold training loss
traces are available via `plot(fit, "loss_trace")` and should
visually level off before the last epoch; if they are still
descending, increase `n_epochs`.

## The estimator runs but `sc_mrs` gives a huge trimmed mean.

The ratio $\hat\beta_j / \hat\beta_k$ is unbounded when the
denominator is near zero. The default trimming at the 1st and 99th
percentiles handles ordinary outliers but a pathological estimator
--- one that puts many respondents near zero on the denominator --- can
still produce a large trimmed mean. Inspect
`plot(fit, "beta_ridgelines")` to confirm the denominator column is
well-separated from zero before interpreting the MRS quantitatively.

## Performance tips

* Set `device = "cuda"` on a GPU machine; bit-exact determinism is
  lost but wall-clock time drops substantially.
* Use `parallel = TRUE` with `n_cores = parallelly::availableCores()`
  on CPU for large datasets --- the package guarantees bit-exact
  identity between parallel and sequential runs.
* Prefer `hidden = "auto"` as a starting point and tune only when a
  clearly-too-small or clearly-too-large architecture is required.
